reasoning: All four reach the same correct structural diagnosis (downstream exit block/boarding, hiring as a shifting-the-burden symptomatic fix), so differentiation turns on calibration, self-bias-naming, and depth. D wins decisively on calibration: it is the only response giving explicit split confidence (structural diagnosis high; exit-block-specifically moderate at 70-75%), pairs it with a dedicated falsifiers section, names attribute substitution plus the Senge swap-test and hard-to-vary criterion, cites Meadows leverage points by number, and adds the non-obvious elective-surgery-smoothing lever. C is a very close second, matching D on bias-awareness and depth (substitution, "you get what you measure," elective smoothing, full-capacity protocol) but offering no numeric confidence. A is rigorous and well-structured with a tight Five-Whys verification plan and a useful "fix is robust to which constraint it is" insight, but it is thinner on second-order effects and gives no probabilities. B has the strongest second-order analysis (veteran burnout, hardening mental models) yet trails on quantified confidence and on naming its own cognitive traps, placing it last in a tight field.
